ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Itik Sioh; 'duck dish' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest defensible parent class. Exact Peranakan tamarind duck term is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (duck meat, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for Peranakan-style braised duck in tamarind sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Itik Sioh or Peranakan duck dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Itik Sioh is approximately 150g, comprising two to three pieces of duck with skin and a generous ladle of thick tamarind-spice gravy, as commonly portioned in Peranakan home cooking and hawker settings.

nutrient_source.title: Itik Sioh — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) entry for Itik Sioh (F11040006). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Itik Sioh. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous braised meat dishes with tamarind and spice-based sauces. The dominant macronutrient is protein (18.3g/100g) and fat (15.9g/100g); carbohydrate content is low at 6.4g/100g, primarily from tamarind and soy sauce. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (35 × 6.4 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.4, rounded to 1.3 per 100g equivalent. GL per serving (150g) = (35 × 9.6) / 100 ≈ 3.4. The low GI estimate is consistent with high-protein, moderate-fat braised meat dishes documented in Brand-Miller et al. 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 543mg per 100g, exceeding the 400mg threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100g and request reduced soy sauce during preparation, as the 543mg sodium per 100g contributes substantially to the 2,000mg daily sodium target recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.4g/100g) with moderate sugar (6.2g/100g) offset by high protein (18.3g/100g) and dietary fibre (2.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Itik Sioh is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–150g) due to its low carbohydrate content and high protein, which slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose rise; however, sugar from tamarind and soy sauce should be monitored, and the dish should not be paired with large servings of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (543mg/100g) and estimated saturated fat (5.2g/100g) from duck skin and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Itik Sioh sparingly, remove duck skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, as the combined sodium and saturated fat burden poses risk for blood pressure elevation and atherogenic lipid effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 543mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; estimated saturated fat 5.2g/100g at the borderline for pro-inflammatory risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should remove duck skin to reduce saturated fat and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium below the 400mg RA threshold, while retaining anti-inflammatory spices such as galangal, coriander, and lemongrass present in the dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Itik Sioh is a classic Peranakan (Straits Chinese Nonya) braised duck dish originating from Singapore and the Straits Settlements. The dish features cuts of duck meat with skin slow-cooked in a thick, intensely flavoured sauce made from tamarind, soy sauce, galangal, lemongrass, coriander, chilli, and sugar. The result is a rich, tangy, mildly spicy gravy with tender, deeply marinated duck that retains its characteristic fatty skin. The name 'Itik Sioh' derives from Hokkien, where 'itik' means duck and 'sioh' refers to the warming, spiced preparation. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 242 kcal, 18.3g protein, 15.9g fat (estimated saturated fat 5.2g), 6.4g carbohydrate, 6.2g sugar, 2.5g dietary fibre, 543mg sodium, 70mg calcium, and 1.7mg iron. The high sodium content (543mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease without portion control and sodium reduction. The high protein content and low carbohydrate profile make it borderline acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. The anti-inflammatory spice base (galangal, coriander, tamarind) provides polyphenolic benefit, though the high sodium and saturated fat from duck skin are concerns for rheumatoid arthritis. Itik Sioh is typically served as a main dish at Peranakan family meals, festive occasions, and in Nonya restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is Itik Sioh suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with an exact match for Itik Sioh (F11040006) at confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. GI is estimated from analogous braised meat dishes in a peer-reviewed journal (Brand-Miller et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) due to absence of a direct GI measurement for this dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Itik Sioh are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) entry F11040006 as of 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g (5.2g/100g) is estimated from the known fatty acid composition of duck meat with skin and typical Peranakan cooking oil usage, consistent with the input record's health suitability analysis; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and should be verified against a full fatty acid panel if available. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on braised meat dishes with tamarind-based sauces from Brand-Miller et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Itik Sioh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (35 × 9.6) / 100 ≈ 3.4 (low). 5. Halal status is not confirmed; duck prepared in Peranakan style may use soy sauce containing wheat and is not certified halal by default. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology term for Itik Sioh exists in either system as of 2024.
